summary: Kenneth advises against selling covered calls immediately after a stock has surged dramatically (e.g., HIMS up 16% in one day). The premiums appear attractive but the risk of a sharp pullback in the following days/weeks makes the risk-reward unfavorable for small premium gains. Instead, he recommends waiting for the inevitable pullback before entering the position.
takeaways: Do not sell covered calls on a stock that has just experienced a massive single-day rip (16%+ moves)
After extreme runups, expect a pullback within days to 1-2 weeks; use Fibonacci retracements to identify likely support levels
Calculate if the premium justifies the capital at risk; $159-$182 premium on $5,800 capital is insufficient risk-reward
When a stock is overbought after a rip, redeploy capital to other stocks with better risk-adjusted premiums rather than forcing a trade on the surged stock
Cash-secured puts on pulled-back stocks offer better entry points than covered calls on fresh runups, if the strike price aligns with your buy target
Wait for the stock to drop to your desired entry price (e.g., $54 on HIMS) before committing capital, rather than chasing after a surge

strategy_candidates.change: Add a rule to skip covered call entries on stocks with >10% single-day moves; redeploy that capital to other positions with better risk-adjusted premiums or wait for pullback confirmation via technical levels (Fibonacci retracements).
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth explicitly states he avoids selling covered calls on surged stocks and instead moves capital elsewhere or waits. He demonstrates Fibonacci retracements as a tool to time re-entry and notes that $159 premium on $5,800 (2.7%) for 2-day hold with pullback risk is poor risk-reward for the Premium Desk's $1,250/mo target.

title: Should you sell covered calls on a stock AFTER it surges?
